coredata.dc:title: Exploring the binding of carbon dots to calf thymus DNA: From green synthesis to fluorescent molecular probe
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Chun-Yan
Xia, Wei
Yang, Cheng-Zhang
Liu, Yong-Chang
Bai, Ai-Min
Hu, Yan-Jun
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have been widely applied in bioimaging, drug delivery and nanomedicine. In this study, the interaction binding mechanism of CDs with calf thymus DNA (ctDNA) has been investigated. A bottom-up synthetic route was selected at low temperature and a one-pot green synthesis of CDs was applied with ascorbic acid as carbon precursor and a water phase reflow at 90 °C under stirring for 3.5 h. The resulting as-synthesized CDs exhibited a green-yellow color, different than what has already been reported for this product with ascorbic acid as carbon precursor. The quantum yield of CDs was 13.3% and the fluorescence decay was 3.19 ns. Analytic methods such as UV-vis absorbance, fluorescence spectroscopy, circular dichroism spectroscopy and electrochemical approaches were used to study the binding mechanism between the CDs and ctDNA. Overall, the results indicated that the binding mode between CDs and ctDNA was intercalation. Moreover, the CDs exhibited specific properties that may be useful in the replacement of other, often toxic fluorescent dyes that are commonly used for organ imaging. CDs may therefore represent a promising fluorescence probe of ctDNA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Calf thymus DNA
Fluorescence probe
Spectroscopy
Electrochemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence characteristics of the CDs-DNA system

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: national natural science foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Hubei key laboratory of pollutant analysis & reuse technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the national natural science foundation of China (No. 21273065 ), the foundation of key laboratory of analytical chemistry for biology and medicine (ministry of education), Wuhan University (No. ACBM2016002 ), and Hubei key laboratory of pollutant analysis & reuse technology, Hubei normal university, China (No. PA150210 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Effects of ratio between AA and PEG-600; (B) Effects of dosage of ascorbic acid with the dosage of PEG-600 is stabilize; (C) Effects of reaction time; (D) Effects of reaction solution pH. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) HR-TEM image and particle size distribution of the as-prepared CDs (inset). (B) Typical single CDs with lattice parameters of 0.205 nm. (C) UV absorption and PL excitation as well as emission spectra of CDs. Photographs of aqueous solution dispersed with CDs under room light (inset, left) and under 365 nm irradiation (inset, right). (D) Emission spectra of CDs with excitation wavelength from 345 nm to 445 nm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The XRD and XPS characterization of CDs. (A) XRD pattern of the as-prepared CDs. (B) XPS full spectra of CDs. (C) XPS spectra of C 1s. (D) XPS spectra of O 1s. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) of the as-synthesized CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Fluorescence decay of the as-synthesized CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

UV absorption spectra of ctDNA with various concentrations of CDs. Comparison of absorption at 260 nm between the ctDNA-CDs complex and sum values of free ctDNA and CDs (inset). c(ctDNA) = 1.0 × 10−4 mol L−1; c(CDs)/(10−4 mol L−1), A-K: from 0 to 9.08 at increments of 0.908. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Fluorescence spectrum of ctDNA with various concentrations of CDs. Inset: Relative fluorescence intensity for ctDNA as a function of CDs for different concentrations; c(ctDNA) = 1.0 × 10−4 mol L−1; c(CDs)/10−3 mol L−1, A-O: from 0 to 12.71 at increments of 0.908. (B) Fluorescence spectra of free CDs and CDs-ctDNA. c(ctDNA) = 1.0 × 10−6 mol L−1, c(CDs) = 4.54 × 10−4 mol L−1. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the CDs-ctDNA system. c(ctDNA)/(10−7 mol L−1), A-K: from 0 to 20 with increments of 2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

CD spectrum of ctDNA with increasing CDs concentration. c(ctDNA) = 1.0 × 10−4 mol L−1, 0.0 represented c(CDs) = 0.0, and c(CDs)/10−4 mol L−1 from 4.8 to 9.6 with increments of 2.4. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Quantum yield of the CDs samples.
Quenching effect of negative ions (I− and [Fe(CN)6]4−) on the CDs-ctDNA interaction.
Stern-Volmer quenching constants of the NR-ctDNA(CDs) system and the EB-ctDNA(CDs) system at various temperatures.
Stern-Volmer quenching constants of the NR-ctDNA(CDs) system and the EB-ctDNA(CDs) system at different NaCl concentrations.
Stern-Volmer quenching constants of the NR-ctDNA(CDs) system and the EB-ctDNA(CDs) system in the absence and presence of a denaturant.
Thermal denaturation effect on the binding of NR-ctDNA(CDs) or EB-ctDNA(CDs) interaction.

head.ce:title: Exploring the binding of carbon dots to calf thymus DNA: From green synthesis to fluorescent molecular probe

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei Normal University, Huangshi 435002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (Ministry of Education), Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (Ministry of Education)

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei Normal University, Huangshi 435002, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been widely applied in bioimaging, drug delivery and nanomedicine. In this study, the interaction binding mechanism of CDs with calf thymus DNA (ctDNA) has been investigated. A bottom-up synthetic route was selected at low temperature and a one-pot green synthesis of CDs was applied with ascorbic acid as carbon precursor and a water phase reflow at 90 °C under stirring for 3.5 h. The resulting as-synthesized CDs exhibited a green-yellow color, different than what has already been reported for this product with ascorbic acid as carbon precursor. The quantum yield of CDs was 13.3% and the fluorescence decay was 3.19 ns. Analytic methods such as UV-vis absorbance, fluorescence spectroscopy, circular dichroism spectroscopy and electrochemical approaches were used to study the binding mechanism between the CDs and ctDNA. Overall, the results indicated that the binding mode between CDs and ctDNA was intercalation. Moreover, the CDs exhibited specific properties that may be useful in the replacement of other, often toxic fluorescent dyes that are commonly used for organ imaging. CDs may therefore represent a promising fluorescence probe of ctDNA.

ce:section.ce:para: In the early 2000s, carbon dots (CDs) have been discovered by Xu and co-workers through the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes derived from an arc-discharge soot [1]. However, due to the traditional semiconductor Quantum dots (QDs) are mainly composed of heavy metals and due to the often high cytotoxicity of fluorescence dyes, and there are obvious limitations in cell and in vivo studied. CDs have become more and more important in the development of carbon nanomaterials because of their generally high biocompatibility, chemical inertness, low toxicity, ease of functionalization, simple synthetic routes, low environmental impact, fluorescence stability, resistance to photobleaching, excitation-wavelength dependent emission, and high electrical conductivity, This list of properties is generally considered advantageous, especially compared to QDs and fluorescence dyes. CDs are useful in numerous industrial applications, including optoelectronic devices, electrocatalyst, photocatalysts, gene delivery, drug delivery, anticancer treatment, bioimaging, biosensing, nanocarbon electrochemistry and electroanalysis. Some of these applications were mimic, compete, or go beyond those already derived from QDs [2–8]. However, the disadvantageous of CDs as described below are apparent. Therefore, for system compare, quantum yield and fluorescence decay of CDs, it is particularly critical to study different types of CDs. Generally, full-color light-emitting is not easy to tune and it is often difficult to produce CDs with controlled size and properties, while the photoluminescence mechanism of CDs is still not fully understood [9–11]. Therefore, obtaining answers to this list of questions remain a critical, albeit unmet, scientific goal.

In recent years, CDs have attracted increasing attention in the scientific community and the synthetic routes typically include two different approaches: top-down methods also include physical methods that generally break down larger carbon structures to reach nanoscale particles, a process that is particularly important in arc discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation, plasma treatment and confined combustion [12,13]. However, these approaches frequently require expensive precursors and synthetic processes as well as post-treatments are often complex. Through chemical methods used in the synthesis of such materials, bottom-up methods are often used in an effort to solve these issues. Bottom-up methods imply that small particles can be produced in a nanoscale form. Examples for such processes include thermal decomposition, microwave or ultrasonic processes, hydrothermal, water phase reflow, and template approaches [14–16]. Due to the economic efficiency and green production as well as simple post-treatment, this article selected the water phase reflow method for the synthesis of CDs.

The interaction of DNA with small molecules represents an important topic in various research fields, including applications in medicinal chemistry, life science, clinical medicine, etc. [17]. The interaction of CDs with biomacromolecules has been studied by various research groups around the globe. Molecular docking has been a helpful tool for the study of small molecule drugs with targeting macromolecules. Presumably, molecular docking may also exhibit suitable properties for future studies involving the interactions between CDs and biomacromolecules. Molecular dynamics simulations have been applied in the study of proteins, and hopefully, this technique may also be applicable for the future study of DNA [18–22]. The binding mode of small molecules to the DNA double helical structure features three major modes of non-covalent interactions, including electrostatic interactions, groove binding and intercalation [23].

CDs exhibit various advantages that are potentially useful in anticancer treatment applications. ctDNA is the carrier of genetic information and represents the target of various drugs in vivo. In this research, the binding modes between CDs and ctDNA have been studied and a bottom-up synthetic route was selected. Compare with the huang's group that the synthesis method for fluorescent carbon dots to label-free and highly selective recognition of DNA, the synthesis approach of this paper that one-pot green synthesis, require low temperature, synthesis processes and carrying out post-treatments are often simple [24]. Via one-pot green synthesis using ascorbic acid as carbon precursor and polyethylene glycol (PEG-600) as surface passivation reagent with water phase reflow at 90 °C, the corresponding CDs could be prepared. The quantum yield of CDs was 13.3%. Nowadays, the fluorescence probes of ctDNA used to study the interaction of ctDNA with a drug are often toxic organic dyes. Therefore, the development of new and nontoxic fluorescent probes remains a crucial, albeit unmet, scientific topic. In doing so, the binding modes of the interaction between CDs and ctDNA could be determined to be due to intercalation. Therefore it is believed that nontoxic CDs may replace organic fluorescent dyes in the future to become a promising fluorescent probe of ctDNA.

Ascorbic acid and polyethylene glycol (PEG-600) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent. Calf thymus DNA was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO). Na2HPO4-NaH2PO4 (PBS) with a purity of no less than 99.5%. All reagents used throughout this study were of analytical purity. The ctDNA solution was dissolved in buffer solution and was stored at 4 °C for one day before further purification. The purity of ctDNA was verified by monitoring the ratio of absorbance at 260/280 nm (A260/A280). The ratio was in the range of 1.8–1.9 as reported previously [25]. Ultrapure water was prepared using deionized water (18.25 MΩ cm−1) and the ultrapurification system EPED-E2-20TJ (Nanjing EPED technology development Co. Ltd, China).

High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were recorded on a JEOL JEM-2100F electron microscope. The crystal structure of the CDs was studied using X-ray diffractometer (XRD) with Cu Kα-radiation (Bruker D8 Advance, Bruker AXS, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on ESCA250Xi (Thermofisher). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was carried out on a Thermo Nicolet iS5 iD1 instrument. UV-visible (UV-vis) absorbance spectra were recorded at room temperature using a UV9000 spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China) equipped with a 1.0 cm quartz cell. Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Perkin-Elmer LS-55 luminescence spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, USA) equipped with a 1.0 cm quartz cells and using a low temperature thermostatic bath (DFY-5/20, Shanghai Wei Kai Instrument Co. Ltd, China). Time-resolved fluorescence spectra were recorded on a FS5 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., UK). Electrochemical experiments were performed on a CHI-660E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instruments Inc., Shanghai, China) using a conventional three-electrode electrochemical testing system.

0.5 g of ascorbic acid and 1.0 g of PEG-600 were mixed and dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water. The mixture was stirred for 10 min at room temperature and was subjected to water phase reflow at 90 °C under stirring for 3.5 h. The emission color of the as-synthesized CDs was green-yellow, different than what has been reported before using ascorbic acid as a carbon precursor [16,26]. The quantum yield of the CDs was 13.3%, considerably higher than the reference value found in the literature [16]. The obtained CDs were dialyzed for 10 h using dialysis membranes with a 500 cutoff. The molar concentration of the CDs was measured on the basis of the mean molecular weight as determined by EIS-MS.

The method to calculate the quantum yield of CDs involved the use of quinine sulfate as a reference. The following equation was used to calculate the quantum yield of the CDs:
(1)
Φ =
Φ R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
η 2
η R 2
here, Φ represents the quantum yield of the sample. Quinine sulfate (ΦR = 0.54) was dissolved in 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4 (refractive index η = 1.33) and the CDs were dissolved in deionized water (η = 1.33). The parameter I represents the integrated emission intensity, with the refractive index η and the optical density A. The subscript R refers to the fluorophore of known quantum yield. To minimize re-absorption effects, the absorption in the 10 mm fluorescence cuvette was kept below 0.1, with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. An excitation and emission slit width of 10 nm and 7.5 nm were used for the sample and fluorescence spectra were recorded within a wavelength range of 380 nm–650 nm.

The UV-vis absorption spectra of ctDNA, CDs alone, as well as the CDs-ctDNA system were measured at a wavelength range of 200 nm–400 nm. Blank buffer solutions and the corresponding sample were placed in the reference and sample cuvettes, respectively. Fluorescence spectra were recorded at room temperature at varying temperatures (298, 304, and 310 K). The slit widths for excitation and emission were both set to 10.0 nm. Titrations were performed manually by using trace syringes. FTIR spectroscopy was carried out using potassium bromide (KBr) pellets and CDs were titrated on the surface of KBr. The CD spectra were obtained with a scanning speed of 50 nm min−1 from 220 to 320 nm at room temperature and under a constant nitrogen flow. Each spectrum was corrected using PBS as a buffer solution.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy with a three-electrode system was performed with a frequency range from 100,000–0.1 Hz, a DC potential of +0.24 V, and an AC amplitude of 5 mV. 5 mL of the electrolyte containing 5 × 10−3 mol L−1 [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and 0.1 mol L−1 KCl were place in the electrochemical cell. The concentration of ctDNA was 1 × 10−4 mol L−1 10 μL of a 6 × 10−2 mol L−1 CDs solution was titrated to the Au electrode and the system was incubated at room temperature. After 4 h, the CDs were titrated to the system again for a total of three times. Different concentrations of the ctDNA solution were added to the CDs/Au electrode system and the solution was stirred for 4 min prior to resting for 4 min before carrying out the analysis.

Generally appreciated is the notion that parameters like the ratio between carbon source and surface passivation reagent, reactant amount of carbon source, time of reaction, pH of reaction, reaction temperature etc. significantly affect the fluorescence intensity of CDs. Furthermore, these parameters significantly affect the experimental fluorescence properties of CDs. Fig. 1 A shows the reaction condition effect of the as-synthesized CDs. The fluorescence intensity was stronger upon increasing the ratio of AA:PEG-600 to 5:3. Furthermore, it was found that the optimum amount of ascorbic acid with the dosage of PEG-600 was stabilize was 0.5 g, Upon decreasing the dosage of ascorbic acid below 0.5 g or upon increasing the amount of ascorbic acid above 0.5 g, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs decreased. The studied reaction times in this study were 2.5 h, 3.0 h, 3.5 h and 4.0 h, respectively. The fluorescence intensity was found to increase gradually to reach a maximum value when the reaction time was increased to 3.5 h. However, the fluorescence intensity decreased upon further increasing the reaction time. From inspection of Fig. 1D it can be determined that the fluorescence intensity of CDs increased if the pH of the reaction solution was below 4.2. However, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased when the pH of the reaction solution was raised to 5.2. The reagent solution of pH was not stable at pH of alkaline, that do not discuss the influence pH of alkaline for synthesis CDs. However, the influence of alkaline pH values in solutions containing carbon dots was discussed below. Finally, the optimized experimental conditions were as follows: AA 0.5 g and PEG-600 1.0 g as carbon precursor and surface passivation respectively were carried out under at pH = 4.2 water phase reflow at 90 °C under stirring for 3.5 h.

The difference of pH influences on the fluorescence properties of CDs in solution have been studied at room temperature. As show in Fig. 1S, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was found to be slightly changed upon changing the solution pH from 4.2 to 10.2. However, the fluorescence intensity changed dramatically upon increasing the solution pH from 4.2 to 2.2 and from 10.2 to 12.2. This result indicated that the fluorescence intensity characteristics of CDs in solution were sensitive to the pH value at meta-acid or leaning alkalinity. However, these characteristics were found to be moderately stable at pH values ranging from 4.2 to 10.2. Influences of temperature variations on the fluorescence properties of CDs in solution have also been studied. The fluorescence intensity characteristics of CDs changed steadily upon changing the temperature from 5 °C to 85 °C (Fig. 2 S). This study illustrates that the fluorescence intensity of CDs were largely temperature dependent.

In an effort to characterize the synthesized CDs, HR-TEM analysis was carried out. As shown in Fig. 2A, the CDs were spherical in shape and monodisperse, with a narrow size distribution. The size distribution, as shown in Fig. 2A (inset), was calculated by measuring dozens of particles and it was determined that the CDs exhibited an average size of 2.16 ± 0.19 nm. As shown in Fig. 2B, lattice spacing of about 0.205 nm could be observed in the crystalline structures of the CDs and the material further exhibited a graphene-like structure. This result is in agreement with results previously reported in the literature [27].

As shown in Fig. 2C (inset), the as-synthesized CDs featured excellent aqueous dispersibility characteristics and further exhibited a distinct green-yellow color under UV irradiation. This latter finding indicated that the CDs possessed suitable optical properties. As shown in Fig. 2C, the characteristic absorption peak at 256 nm of the CDs in the UV-vis spectrum may be attributed to the n-π* transition of the C=O band and the π-π* transition of the conjugated C=C band [28]. CDs exhibit strong UV absorption properties with a decreasing intensity absorption tail extending into the visible light region. This particular optical property may be useful for potential applications of CDs in bio-related applications [18]. The fluorescence spectra display an emission maximum at 445 nm, with an excitation wavelength of 375 nm (cf. Fig. 2C). And Fig. 2D shows that upon varying the excitation wavelength from 345 to 445 nm, the emission peak shifted from 444 nm to 504.5 nm and the fluorescence peak intensity first increased and then gradually decreased. This result indicated that the excitation wavelength exhibited by CDs was mostly emission dependent and the emission peak was symmetric. This feature may also allow the use of CDs for multicolor imaging applications by modifying the excitation wavelength [18]. This optical property sets the foundation for CDs to potentially replace organic dyes in bio-related applications.

The XRD results shown in Fig. 3 A feature three broad peaks around 18.8°, 29.9°, 41.1°, indicating the presence of amorphous carbon particles and the (002) and (100) graphite lattice [6,29]. XPS is a sensitive analysis method to study the material surface that provided the surface elemental composition and surface function groups. From the full spectra of CDs shown in Fig. 3B, two apparent peaks at 285.2 eV and 531.8 eV are attributed to C 1s and O 1s, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3C, The high-resolution spectra C 1s band can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.6 eV, 285.6 eV and 286.7 eV representing C=C, C−C and C−OH, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3D, The O 1s band can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.6 and at 533.0 eV representing C=O and C−O, respectively [9,30]. FTIR spectroscopy may provide information on the functional groups and the structure of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 4 , the broad and intense peak at around 3408 cm−1 may be attributed to the stretching vibrations of O−H, indicating that hydroxyl groups were present as surface functional groups of CDs. Concurrently, the peak at 1455 cm−1 and 1351 cm−1 indicated the presence of COO− groups. As a consequence, the as-prepared CDs exhibited a high water solubility. The peak at 1721.62 cm−1 could be attributed to the stretching vibrations of C=O. The stretching vibrations for C−H, C=C, C−C, C−O were found at 2872 cm−1, 1632 cm−1, 1250 cm−1, 1107 cm−1, respectively.

As displayed in Table 1 , using quinine sulfate (54%, 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4) as a reference, the fluorescence quantum yield of CDs could be calculated to be 13.3% using equation (1) [31]. The characteristic period of the CDs remains in its excited state prior to returning to its ground state throughout the fluorescence lifetime. Fluorescence lifetime represents an intrinsic property of CDs and largely depends on the nature of the fluorescence sites and the environment. The fluorescence lifetime of CDs could be measured by time-resolved fluorescence. The decay trace was fitted using biexponential functions Y (t) based on a non-linear least squares analysis using equation (2):
(2)
Y
( t )
=
A 1
e x p
(
− t
/
τ 1
)
+
A 2
e x p
(
− t
/
τ 2
)
(3)
τ
a v e r a g e
=(
Α 1
τ 1 2
+
Α 2
τ 2 2
)/(
Α 1
τ 1
+
Α 2
τ 2
)
here, A1 and A2 represent the fractional contributions of the time-resolved decay lifetime of τ1 and τ2. The biexponential behavior of the lifetime suggested that two different emission sites were present. In turn, this suggested that the fluorescence state was due to the graphite center (or conjugated structures) as well as the surface traps [31]. Using equation (3), the average fluorescence lifetime could be calculated.

As show in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence lifetime of the CDs was 3.19 ns. This short lifetime indicated that the photoluminescent mechanism may be due to the radioactive recombination of excitations [32]. The molecular mass of CDs was measured using a LCQ ADVANTAGE (Thermo Finnigan corporation) EIS-MS. The molecular weight of CDs was about 696, a value that is similar with the one previously reported in the literature [19].

In general, the two major features of DNA absorption spectra are hyperchromic and hypochromic effects due to changes in the double helical structure. Hyperchromism takes place due to the breakage of the secondary structure of the DNA duplex. Hypochromism originates from the stabilization of the DNA duplex by either an intercalation binding mode or an electrostatic effect of small molecules [33]. In this context, the fixed concentration of ctDNA and the change of UV-vis absorption spectra were recorded with an increasing amount of CDs. As shown in Fig. 6 , the maximum absorption peak of blank ctDNA was 260 nm and the absorption peak increased upon increasing the amount of CDs. Furthermore, the maximum absorption exhibited a slight red shift. The inset in Fig. 6 shows that a hypochromic affect existed in the CDs sample with ctDNA due to the fact that the sum of the absorption value of free ctDNA and free CDs was greater than the absorption value of the ctDNA-CDs complex. This result indicated that the binding mode between ctDNA and CDs was intercalation and other evidence further supported this conclusion as discussed below.

In general, intercalation bound small molecules shall be protected from being quenched by anionic quencher, whereas the free buffered solution complexes and groove binding drugs may be quenched readily by anionic quenchers [34]. To further elucidate the binding mode of CDs with ctDNA, potassium iodide (KI) and potassium hexacyanoferrate(II) (K4[Fe(CN)6]) were used as quencher species. The fluorescence quenching in the absence and presence of DNA by KI and K4[Fe(CN)6] was studied. As show in Fig. S3 the obtained fluorescence quenching data was analyzed using the Stern-Volmer equation as follows [35]:
(4)
F 0
F
= 1 + K sv
[ Q ]
here, F0 and F denote the steady-state fluorescence intensities in the absence and in the presence of a quencher species (KI or K4[Fe(CN)6]). K
SV represents the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and [Q] is the concentration of the quencher. Equation (4) was applied to determine K
SV by linear regression of a plot of F0/F against [Q]. The Stern-Volmer plots for the CDs quenched by I− and [Fe(CN)6]4− ions in the absence and presence of ctDNA (cf. Fig. S3) and the quenching constant (K SV) values can be found listed in Table 2 . Here, the negatively charged quenching effect was found to be decreased upon addition of ctDNA, providing further evidence for the notion that CDs molecules intercalated into the base pairs of ctDNA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence characteristics of the CDs-DNA system
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence spectroscopy is a commonly used technique for the study of interactions between small molecules and ctDNA. As show in Fig. 7 A, the stabilized concentration of ctDNA with increasing concentration of CDs resulted in a gradual increase of the fluorescence intensity. The inset in Fig. 7A also shows that at a concentration of CDs higher than 12.71 mol L−1, the fluorescence intensity tended to remain constant. From inspection of Fig. 7B it can be concluded that a characteristic fluorescence signal of CDs appeared at ∼445 nm when the excitation wavelength was stabilized at 375 nm. The fluorescence intensity of CDs was obviously quenched by the addition of DNA, further indicating an interaction between DNA and CDs. Moreover, this finding is in agreement with literature data published previously [36].

It has already been shown that ethidium bromide (EB) and neutral red (NR) represent classic intercalation fluorescence probes. Both species may be used in the competitive displacement of the binding mode of an unknown molecule with ctDNA, similarly to known intercalators where the fluorescence intensity of a ctDNA-intercalator decreases. Addition of NR or EB to the CDs-ctDNA system may result in a replacement of the CDs molecules and may ultimately lead to the formation of more stable NR-ctDNA or EB-ctDNA complexes. Fig. S4 shows that the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-ctDNA complex decreased significantly upon addition of EB or NR. This finding is indicative of the binding mode of CDs to ctDNA and is in agreement with NR and EB as discussed in the literature [37]. Furthermore, this finding provides further evidence for the intercalation ability of CDs molecules into the base pairs of ctDNA.

Dynamic and static quenching events can be distinguished by differing the dependence on temperature and viscosity [38]. Higher temperatures generally result in faster diffusion and hence larger amounts of dynamic quenching. Higher temperatures typically result in the dissociation of weakly bound complexes so that smaller amounts of static quenching may be observed. Static quenching generally results from the formation of a complex between a quencher species and a fluorophore in the ground state. However, dynamic quenching can be observed by interaction of a quencher species and a fluorophore through dynamic collision. Thus, through calculation were able to determine the Stern-Volmer quenching constant K
SV at different temperatures to distinguish between different quenching types. Fig. S5 shows the Stern-Volmer plots of the fluorescence quenching of the ctDNA-CDs system by addition of NR or EB at different temperatures (298, 304, 310 K). The corresponding Stern-Volmer quenching constants K
SV at different temperatures are shown in Table 3 . The results obtained show that upon using CDs as a fluorescence probe, the quenching mechanism in the interaction of NR with ctDNA follows dynamic quenching since the quenching constants increased with higher temperature. On the contrary, the quenching mechanism of EB in the binding interaction with ctDNA resulted in the formation of a ground state compound rather than by dynamic collision.

To distinguish the binding modes between small molecules and ctDNA, the determination of the ionic strength effect represents a powerful method. Each of the fluorophores may carry a positive charge and DNA features a negative polyphosphate backbone. An increased ionic strength generally results in phosphate-phosphate repulsion, prompting the helix to shrink due to the decreased unwinding tendency caused by electrostatic repulsion between the negatively charged phosphate groups [39]. The quenching constants of the NR-ctDNA (CDs) system and the EB-ctDNA(CDs) system are shown in Table 4 . Here, it can be determined that the quenching constants increased in the absence of NaCl. This finding proves that a very high concentration of NaCl hindered NR or EB from binding to ctDNA. This provides further evidence for the intercalation binding mode of CDs with ctDNA.

Two classical chemical denaturation agents are urea and guanidine hydrochloride (GuHCl), known to be able to denaturate the noncovalent interactions of protein and nucleic acids [40]. We introduced urea and GuHCl to the NR-ctDNA(CDs) system and the EB-ctDNA(CDs) system in an effort to study the corresponding binding effects. The data obtained was analyzed via the Stern-Volme method as shown in Table 5 . The quenching constants were found to decrease upon addition of urea and GuHCl to DNA. Furthermore, the quenching constants of GuHCl decreased obviously compared with urea. This finding demonstrates that urea and GuHCl both resulted in the denaturation of DNA. In this case, the replacement of the fluorescent probe CDs with NR or EB to intercalate with DNA was possible, further providing evidence for the intercalation binding mode of CDs with DNA.

Thermal denaturation is an effective way to provide data on the binding mode of small molecules with DNA. The melting temperature (Tm) is described as the temperature at which 50% of double stranded DNA becomes single stranded. The Tm of native DNA is about 75.9 ± 0.3 °C. This temperature increases in the presence of intercalators. When all intercalating sites are saturated, the Tm of DNA increases about 5 °C. On the contrary, groove binding does not lead to an appreciable change in the Tm value [41]. As shown in Fig. S6 and Table 6 , the Stern-Volmer quenching constants of the NR-ctDNA(CDs) system or the EB-ctDNA(CDs) system were found to be maximum at 80 °C. This result indicated that both NR and EB served as intercalators, further providing evidence for the intercalation binding mode of CDs with ctDNA.

Electrochemical studied are generally used for probe DNA sensing and in the study of interactions between single-stranded DNA and double-stranded DNA [42,43]. In this paper, the EIS was selected to discuss interactions between CDs and ctDNA. The ability to detect interfacial properties at the electrode surface is critically important. Therefore, EIS has become an increasingly versatile and efficient method for the highly sensitive evaluation of electron-transfer processes [44]. In general, if the diameter of the semicircle in the Nyquist plots increases, an introduced substance hinders electron-transfer processes in the electrode surface. Fig. 8 shows that the diameter of the semicircle in the Nyquist plots increased with increasing concentrations of ctDNA. This suggests that upon titration of ctDNA to the CDs system, CDs electrons are impeded in the electrode surface transfer, a finding that is mostly due to the formation of a complex between ctDNA and CDs.

Conformational changes of DNA can be effectively identified using circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy. CD spectra of the DNA duplex displayed a canonical B-form conformation, characterized by a positive band at 275 nm due to the base stacking interactions of DNA. Furthermore, a negative band at 241 nm can be seen due to the right–handed helicity of DNA. Groove binding or electrostatic interactions of small molecules with DNA do not greatly impact the base stacking and the helicity characteristics of the B-form of DNA. However, intercalation between DNA and small molecules has been found to generally increase the base stacking and stabilize the right handed helicity causing positive and negative signal changes [45]. Fig. 9 shows that the positive and negative CD signals of ctDNA were disturbed upon increasing the concentration of CDs, indicating that the base stacking and right-hand helicity were also perturbed. This latter result provides further evidence for the intercalation binding mode between CDs and ctDNA.

In this study, CDs were prepared that emit green-yellow light, different than CDs that have been synthesized in the past using ascorbic acid as carbon precursor. Compared to organic dyes, the as-prepared CDs featured properties such as small size, high QY, good dispersibility, excitation wavelength dependent emission, and suitable optical properties. The fluorescence intensity stability was found to be highest at pH values ranging between 4.2 and 10.2 and was also found to be temperature dependent. Through the study of UV-vis spectra, iodide quenching experiments, competitive displacement assays, ionic strength effects and chemical denaturation circular dichroism spectroscopy, the binding modes and the mechanism of the interaction between CDs and ctDNA were determined to be due to intercalation. Taken in concert, the experimental results on the properties of CDs obtained in this study may potentially be useful to replace organic dyes in clinical applications. Potentially, CDs may be used in future applications as a new fluorescence probe to study the interaction between a drug and ctDNA in a more efficient fashion.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the national natural science foundation of China (No. 21273065), the foundation of key laboratory of analytical chemistry for biology and medicine (ministry of education), Wuhan University (No. ACBM2016002), and Hubei key laboratory of pollutant analysis & reuse technology, Hubei normal university, China (No. PA150210).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Supplementary data Supplementary data

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2018.01.009.
